title: Sunday Morning at the Flea Market
summary: A Brooklyn narrator describes her favourite Sunday ritual: walking through the local flea market, looking at old things, and rarely buying anything.
body: Every Sunday morning, I walk down to the flea market two blocks from my apartment in Brooklyn. I usually arrive around nine, when the sellers are still drinking coffee and setting out their tables. I'm not really there to shop. I go because the flea market feels like a small history museum that nobody planned. Last week, I saw a brown leather suitcase from the nineteen-fifties. The label inside said its owner once travelled to Berlin. There was also a pile of old postcards, some of them written in beautiful handwriting and others almost impossible to read. One postcard said, 'Dear Mama, the sea is cold but I am happy.' I stood there for a long time, wondering who wrote it and whether Mama ever wrote back. I rarely buy anything. Maybe once a month I take home a small object — a wooden spoon, a glass jar, a postcard with a kind message. The seller who knows me best is an old man named Frank. He tells me stories about each piece, even when I'm only looking. Last Sunday, he asked, 'Why do you come if you don't buy?' I told him I come for the stories, not the things. He smiled and said that was the best reason of all.

annotations.exampleEn: She bought an old lamp at the flea market.
The café is only two blocks from here.
The sellers were setting out their tables before the shoppers arrived.
He carried a brown leather suitcase.
The label inside the bag had a name.
There was a pile of old postcards on the table.

annotations.meaningVi: gần như không thể đọc nổi
annotations.exampleEn: Some of the notes were almost impossible to read.

annotations.meaningVi: tự hỏi ai đã viết nó
annotations.exampleEn: I was wondering who wrote the song.

annotations.meaningVi: lý do hay nhất trong tất cả
annotations.exampleEn: He said love was the best reason of all.

grammarNotes.title: Adverbs of frequency: usually, sometimes, rarely
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Các trạng từ tần suất (usually, often, sometimes, rarely, never) thường đứng trước động từ thường, sau động từ to be. Trong bài: 'I usually arrive around nine' và 'I rarely buy anything.'
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Adverbs of frequency (usually, often, sometimes, rarely, never) go before the main verb and after the verb 'to be'. From the text: 'I usually arrive around nine' and 'I rarely buy anything.'

grammarNotes.title: Past simple to narrate a memorable moment
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Dùng thì quá khứ đơn để kể chuỗi sự kiện trong một lần ghé thăm cụ thể. Trong bài: 'Last week, I saw a brown leather suitcase…', 'One postcard said…', 'I stood there for a long time.'
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Use Past Simple to narrate a specific past event. From the text: 'Last week, I saw a brown leather suitcase…', 'One postcard said…', 'I stood there for a long time.'

translationVi: Mỗi sáng Chủ nhật, tôi đi bộ đến phiên chợ trời cách căn hộ của tôi ở Brooklyn hai dãy nhà. Tôi thường đến lúc chín giờ, khi mấy người bán vẫn đang uống cà phê và bày bàn ra. Tôi không thật sự đến để mua sắm. Tôi đến vì chợ trời giống như một bảo tàng lịch sử nhỏ mà chẳng ai sắp đặt cả. Tuần trước, tôi thấy một chiếc va-li da nâu từ những năm năm mươi. Cái nhãn bên trong cho biết chủ cũ từng đi Berlin. Còn có một chồng bưu thiếp cũ, vài cái nét chữ rất đẹp, vài cái gần như không đọc nổi. Một tấm bưu thiếp có dòng: 'Mẹ ơi, biển lạnh nhưng con vui.' Tôi đứng đó rất lâu, tự hỏi ai đã viết nó và liệu Mama có hồi đáp không. Tôi hiếm khi mua gì. Khoảng một tháng một lần, tôi đem về một món nhỏ — cái thìa gỗ, một chiếc lọ thuỷ tinh, một tấm bưu thiếp có lời nhắn tử tế. Người bán quen tôi nhất là một ông cụ tên Frank. Ông kể tôi nghe câu chuyện về từng món đồ, kể cả khi tôi chỉ ngắm. Chủ nhật trước, ông hỏi: 'Sao cô đến mà không mua?' Tôi nói rằng tôi đến vì những câu chuyện, không phải vì những món đồ. Ông cười và nói đó là lý do hay nhất trong tất cả.

questions.prompt: When does the narrator visit the flea market?
questions.explanation: 'Every Sunday morning, I walk down to the flea market.'
questions.hint: Listen for the day and time at the start.

questions.prompt: How far is the market from her apartment?
questions.explanation: 'two blocks from my apartment in Brooklyn.'
questions.hint: Listen for a number of blocks.

questions.prompt: What are the sellers usually doing when she arrives?
options: Drinking coffee and setting out their tables
questions.explanation: 'the sellers are still drinking coffee and setting out their tables.'

questions.prompt: What does the flea market feel like to her?
options: A small history museum that nobody planned
questions.explanation: 'the flea market feels like a small history museum that nobody planned.'

questions.prompt: What old object did she see last week?
options: A brown leather suitcase from the 1950s
questions.explanation: 'I saw a brown leather suitcase from the nineteen-fifties.'
questions.hint: Listen for a travel item with a decade.

questions.prompt: Where had the suitcase's owner once travelled, according to the label?
What did the postcard from the sea say?
options: 'Dear Mama, the sea is cold but I am happy'
questions.explanation: 'One postcard said, Dear Mama, the sea is cold but I am happy.'

questions.prompt: What was she wondering as she read the postcard?
options: Who wrote it and whether Mama ever wrote back
questions.explanation: 'wondering who wrote it and whether Mama ever wrote back.'
questions.hint: Listen for two questions she had in her mind.

questions.prompt: How often does she buy something?
questions.explanation: 'Maybe once a month I take home a small object.'
questions.hint: Listen for the time frame of her purchases.

options: The old seller who tells her stories
questions.explanation: 'The seller who knows me best is an old man named Frank.'
questions.hint: Listen for who Frank is in relation to her.

questions.prompt: What did the narrator tell Frank when he asked why she comes?
options: She comes for the stories, not the things
questions.explanation: 'I told him I come for the stories, not the things.'
questions.hint: Listen for her own reason near the end.

cloze.template: Every Sunday morning, I walk down to the flea {{b1}} two blocks from my apartment in Brooklyn. I usually arrive around nine, when the sellers are still drinking coffee and {{b2}} out their tables. I'm not really there to shop. I go because the flea market feels like a small {{b3}} museum that nobody planned. Last week, I saw a brown leather {{b4}} from the nineteen-fifties. The label inside said its owner once travelled to {{b5}}. There was also a pile of old postcards, some of them written in beautiful {{b6}} and others almost impossible to read. One postcard said, 'Dear Mama, the sea is cold but I am happy.' I stood there for a long time, wondering who wrote it and whether Mama ever wrote back. I {{b7}} buy anything. Maybe once a month I take home a small object — a wooden spoon, a glass jar, a postcard with a kind message. The seller who knows me best is an old man named Frank. He tells me stories about each piece, even when I'm only looking. Last Sunday, he asked, 'Why do you come if you don't buy?' I told him I come for the {{b8}}, not the things. He {{b9}} and said that was the best {{b10}} of all.

blanks.explanation: 'market' — 'flea market' is the place she visits.
'setting' — 'setting out' means putting things in place.
'history' — full of old objects from the past.
'suitcase' — the brown leather travel bag from the 1950s.
'Berlin' — the city the owner once visited.
'handwriting' — the way people wrote with a pen.
'stories' — what Frank shares about each item.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The narrator says she comes 'for the stories, not the things'. When you walk through an old market or a second-hand shop, do you also feel that objects carry stories with them? Why might one used cup feel different from a brand-new one?
